His sisters' graves share the Yates family burial plot
Both lived in Honolulu's missionary elite for decades

narrative_blocks.header: A Schenectady parsonage birth, a Hawaiian royal death
narrative_blocks.body: John Owen Dominis was born on March 10, 1832 at 26 Front Street in Schenectady — the home of Reverend Andrew Yates, whose wife Hannah Allin Hooker was a descendant of Rev. Thomas Hooker, founder of Hartford, Connecticut; the Dominis family, headed by an Italian-American sea captain trading the Pacific, had moved to Schenectady around 1831 and boarded with the Yates household. He died on August 27, 1891 at Washington Place in Honolulu as Prince Consort of the Kingdom of Hawaiʻi — the kingdom's last and only male consort — having traveled the full arc from a minister's parsonage in upstate New York to the Hawaiian royal court.

narrative_blocks.header: Climbed the fence to meet Hawaii's future queen
narrative_blocks.body: In late May 1843, at age eleven, John Owen Dominis was admitted to the Chiefs' Children's School (Royal School) in Honolulu by Amos and Juliette Cooke — the same Juliette Cooke for whom the Hooker-line missionary Samuel Gelston Dwight named his daughter Juliette Cooke Dwight. Before his admission, Dominis had attended a day school whose yard abutted the Royal School's fence; he used to climb it to look at the princes and princesses, and became their friend — including a girl named Lydia Kamakaʻeha who would one day be Queen Liliʻuokalani and his wife.

narrative_blocks.header: Governor of Oahu for every remaining Hawaiian monarch
narrative_blocks.body: Appointed Royal Governor of Oahu by Kamehameha V in 1864, John Owen Dominis held that office through the reigns of every subsequent Hawaiian monarch — simultaneously governing Maui, Lanai, and Molokai from 1878, serving on the Privy Council, Board of Health, Board of Education, and Bureau of Immigration, and ultimately being named Lieutenant General and Commander in Chief in 1886. His obituary noted he was 'the contemporary of five reigning sovereigns, with every one of whom he exercised a strong influence' — a remarkable civic career for the boy born in a Schenectady clergyman's home.

narrative_blocks.header: His mother's boardinghouse became Hawaii's royal palace
narrative_blocks.body: After Captain Dominis was lost at sea in 1846, his widow Mary rented rooms in their Honolulu home; a boarder — the US Minister to Hawaii — named it 'Washington Place,' approved by Kamehameha III in 1848. The house where a Schenectady sea captain's wife once took in boarders passed to John Owen on his mother's death in 1889, then to Queen Liliuokalani on his death in 1891, and finally became the official governor's residence of Hawaii — it is now a museum on Beretania Street, Honolulu.

quotes.text: The lamented Prince was the contemporary of five reigning sovereigns, with every one of whom he exercised a strong influence. His counsels were born of an excellent judgment and he had rare tact in impressing others with his views.
